ground_truth.title: Building Defenses Against Cyber Risk in the Construction Sector
ground_truth.author: David Anderson, Bridget Quinn Choi, Jamin Valdez
ground_truth.main_content: The cyber risks the construction industry is exposed to are a mix of common cyberattacks as well as very niche risks that can permeate every corner of a construction enterprise. From building neighborhood homes to mile-long suspension bridges, construction is the foundation of the growth and essential to the development and advancement of the country. Because of the importance of the construction industry, it is imperative that we understand the existential threats presented by cyberattacks and cyber criminals to construction companies, contractors, and their employees. The construction industry has always inherently had cyber risk. For years, cybersecurity experts have warned contractors that they are targets for ransomware attacks, phishing theft, and data breaches/theft of sensitive information. Today, construction industry industrial espionage and geopolitically driven cyber disruption are on the rise. The construction industry has experienced massive losses including stolen or misdirected funds and failed bids due to system interruptions, as well as brand damage to future teaming arrangements, lost contracts, and customer confidence, on top of cascading cyber incident response costs like system restoration and ransom payments. There are several factors present in the construction industry that make it more attractive to criminals. Lack of investment in cyber security infrastructure means many engineering and construction companies have not properly invested in cyber security and pay dearly when they experience an attack. The industry is a target for those seeking sensitive information like proprietary construction plans and designs, facilities security information, and other intellectual property. Fast adoption of new technologies has paved the way for increased productivity but cyber and data privacy risk is often overlooked in the race to embrace new technologies. Reliance on legacy systems is a significant problem as operating systems that are no longer supported have known vulnerabilities with no patches available. Third-party risk includes potential data breaches due to vulnerabilities within a vendor's IT environment. Basic cybersecurity measures include multi-factor authentication for all remote access, webmail, and privileged accounts; employee training with robust phishing simulations; strict dual controls with callback requirements for payment account modifications; effective data breach prevention strategies; endpoint detection and response; software sandboxing; segmented and protected backups; and a tested incident response plan.
with: The cyber risks the construction industry is exposed to are a mix of common cyberattacks as well as very niche risks that can permeate every corner of a construction enterprise.

The construction industry has always inherently had cyber risk. For years, cybersecurity experts have warned contractors that they are targets for ransomware attacks, phishing theft, and data breaches/theft of sensitive information.

Many engineering and construction companies operate on narrow margins. Effective and technology implementation and the accompanying data privacy and security compliance require dedicated corporate resources.
Legacy or end-of-life operating systems present significant opportunities for cybercriminals because support has ended and patches will not be available.

Taking measures to ensure adequate controls are in place to protect the enterprise's ability to function includes multi-factor authentication for all remote access, webmail, and privileged and administrative accounts.
